output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: I'm flagging substantive problems with the framing — particularly overclaimed novelty and slippery terminology — and proposing a revised introduction that lands the contribution more defensibly.

The biggest weakness is the novelty claim. "First method that equips a vision transformer with an internal self-evaluation head" and "first introspective head architecture for ViTs" almost certainly will not survive review. A two-layer MLP on the [CLS] token trained to predict correctness is, mechanically, a **confidence/failure-prediction head**, and that literature is large and predates ViTs:

- Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017), "A Baseline for Detecting Misclassified and Out-of-Distribution Examples" — the canonical MSP baseline you are comparing against.
- DeVries & Taylor (2018), "Learning Confidence for Out-of-Distribution Detection in Neural Networks" — explicitly adds a learned confidence head.
- Corbière et al. (NeurIPS 2019), "Addressing Failure Prediction by Learning Model Confidence" (ConfidNet) — a learned failure-prediction head trained on a held-out split, which is essentially what SelfProbe is.
- Jiang et al. (2018), "Trust Score"; Granese et al. (2021), "DOCTOR"; and the broader selective-classification line (Geifman & El-Yaniv, 2017/2019).

You must cite and differentiate against these. The honest delta is probably: (a) applied to ViTs specifically, (b) demonstrated under ImageNet-C shift, (c) the [CLS]-token attachment point. That is a reasonable contribution; do not inflate it.

Second, the "know what it knows" framing borrowed from the LM literature (Kadavath et al., 2022) is rhetorically loaded. A held-out MLP is not introspection in any meaningful sense — it is a second classifier reading the same features. I'd retire the introspection/self-evaluation language or, at minimum, define it operationally and acknowledge the framing is metaphorical.

Third, evidence gaps the abstract should address or hedge:
- AUROC 0.847 vs 0.781 is reported without a baseline beyond MSP. Temperature scaling, ODIN, energy scores, and ConfidNet are the obvious comparators; without them the 0.847 number is uninterpretable.
- Single seed? Confidence intervals? On ImageNet-C, severity 3 only — why not the standard mean-over-severities?
- The selective-prediction "3.1-point accuracy gain" needs a coverage-risk curve, not a single operating point, and should be compared against MSP-routing AUC of the risk-coverage curve (the standard metric from Geifman & El-Yaniv).
- "Frozen ViT-B/16" — frozen on what pretraining? Supervised IN-1k? DINO? This materially affects whether [CLS] carries useful uncertainty signal.

Fourth, prose-level issues: "widely known to be miscalibrated" is hand-wavy (cite Guo et al., 2017; Minderer et al., 2021 for ViTs specifically). "Substantially above" should be replaced by the delta and a significance statement. "Novel primitive for trustworthy vision systems" is marketing — cut.

Suggested revision of the opening framing:

> Vision transformers trained for image classification are poorly calibrated, especially under distribution shift (Guo et al., 2017; Minderer et al., 2021), and downstream systems that abstain or defer on uncertain inputs need a reliable per-example correctness signal. The dominant baseline — thresholding the maximum softmax probability (MSP; Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2017) — is known to degrade under shift, and learned failure-prediction heads such as ConfidNet (Corbière et al., 2019) have been shown to improve over MSP on CNNs. Whether the same approach transfers to ViTs, and in particular whether the [CLS]-token representation carries correctness-relevant information beyond what the classifier head extracts, is unsettled.
>
> We present SelfProbe, a learned failure-prediction head for frozen ViT-B/16. SelfProbe is a two-layer MLP attached to the [CLS] token, trained on a held-out ImageNet split to predict whether the backbone's top-1 prediction is correct. We evaluate against MSP, temperature-scaled MSP, and a ConfidNet-style head on ResNet-50 as a control. On ImageNet-Val, SelfProbe reaches AUROC 0.847 (vs. MSP 0.781, [TS-MSP X.XXX], [ConfidNet-style X.XXX]); on ImageNet-C averaged across severities 1–5, SelfProbe reaches AUROC 0.[XXX] (vs. MSP 0.[XXX]). In a selective-prediction setting, SelfProbe improves the AURC over MSP by [X]% and yields a 3.1-point accuracy gain at 80% coverage.
>
> Our contribution is not the head architecture, which is standard, but evidence that (i) [CLS]-token features in a ViT contain correctness signal not recoverable from softmax, (ii) this signal is robust to ImageNet-C corruption, and (iii) it transfers to selective-prediction gains.

This framing concedes the methodological precedent and bets the contribution on the empirical findings — which is defensible.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the exact titles/years of Corbière et al., DeVries & Taylor, Granese et al., or Minderer et al. against the literature in this session; please confirm before citing. The specific numeric values I bracketed ([X.XXX], etc.) are placeholders for measurements you would need to run.
